topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled paper felt like a live wire in my hand. It was my essay, the one I’d poured my entire soul into, now printed on glossy, cream-colored stock, sitting next to a perfectly formatted sample from the university’s admissions website. My breath hitched, shallow and fast, catching somewhere in my chest. I reread the sentences, the carefully chosen metaphors, and the sheer, terrifying realization washed over me: this wasn't just *my* story; it was a blueprint, a template, a ghost of a success I was now accidentally holding. My hands started trembling so violently I had to clamp them over my mouth to stop any sound escaping. I dropped the paper onto the desk with a soft thud, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the silent dorm room. I stood up, pacing the small space, my gaze darting frantically between the pristine example and my own messy draft, a dizzying spiral beginning in my stomach.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my vision blurring slightly around the edges. The text glowed, flawless and polished, exactly how the guidance counselor had described the ideal narrative. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that seemed to vibrate through my entire frame. I leaned closer, my nose almost touching the glass, trying to absorb the structure, the cadence, the effortless elegance of the prose. A hot flush crept up my neck, turning my cheeks bright red. I started shaking my head, a rapid, jerky motion, trying to dislodge the image, to scrub the perfection from my mind. "No," I whispered, the word thin and reedy. I snatched the laptop away as if burned, stumbling back against the wall. My chest felt tight, constricted, as if someone had clamped a vise around my lungs, and I could only manage ragged, gulping breaths.

[story 3]

It was tucked inside a discarded textbook, a misplaced piece of campus literature. When I unfolded it, the words swam into focus, and the world seemed to tilt. Every carefully constructed sentence mirrored my own internal monologue, yet it felt alien, borrowed. A wave of intense disorientation crashed over me. I began to breathe in deep, ragged gasps, sucking in air as if trying to fill a void. My fingers curled into tight fists, the knuckles white against the paper. I paced the length of my cramped apartment, my eyes wide and unfocused, scanning the room as if expecting the template to materialize and judge me. A low, uncontrolled sound escaped my throat—a choked, high-pitched noise that quickly dissolved into a series of unsteady, rapid breaths. I needed to move, to escape the suffocating proximity of that perfect writing.
